g.data-narration: Welcome to Introduction to Data Structures. This topic forms the foundation of efficient computing.
A data structure organizes and stores data for efficient access and modification. It is about structuring data to enable fast operations.
g.text: A way to organize, store, and manage data for efficient access and modification.
Enables fast operations: access, search, insert, delete

g.data-narration: The choice of data structure determines algorithm efficiency. Searching 10000 names can take 10000 operations or just 1, depending on structure.
An ADT defines what operations are supported without specifying implementation. It is a logical model focusing on behavior, not how it works.
ADT is the logical concept while data structure is the physical implementation. List ADT can be array or linked list.
Data structures classify into primitive and non-primitive types. Non-primitive further divides into linear and non-linear structures.
Primitive data types are basic types provided by programming languages. They hold single values: int, float, char, and double.
g.text: Basic data types provided by programming language - hold single values:

g.data-narration: Linear structures arrange elements sequentially. Each element connects to previous and next. Examples include arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues.
g.text: Elements arranged sequentially - each element has one predecessor and one successor.

g.data-narration: Arrays store elements of the same type in contiguous memory. They provide O(1) access by index but require O(n) for insertions.
Linked lists store elements in nodes with data and pointer. They allow dynamic size with O(1) insertions at known positions.
g.text: Dynamic size, non-contiguous nodes with pointers:
struct Node { int data; struct Node* next; }

g.data-narration: Stacks follow LIFO - Last In, First Out. Think of a stack of plates. Operations push and pop happen at one end called the top.
g.text: Last In, First Out - like a stack of plates:

g.data-narration: Queues follow FIFO - First In, First Out. Like a ticket line. Insert at rear, remove from front. Essential for scheduling.
g.text: First In, First Out - like a ticket line:

g.data-narration: Trees are hierarchical with root and children. Binary Search Trees organize so left subtree has smaller, right has larger values.
g.text: Hierarchical structure with root and child nodes:

g.data-narration: Graphs have vertices connected by edges. Used for networks, maps, social connections. Can be directed or undirected.
g.text: G = (V, E) vertices connected by edges:

g.data-narration: Data structures and algorithms are inseparable. Structure choice determines which algorithms work and their time complexity.
g.text: Structure choice determines algorithm applicability and complexity:

g.data-narration: Key points: Data structures organize data efficiently. ADTs define behavior without implementation. Structures classify into primitive and non-primitive.
Remember: Know definitions, classification tree, ADT vs structure difference, time complexities, and real-world examples for exams.
g.text: Linked List: Insert O(1)*, Search O(n)
